serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of brewed tomato seaweed soup is approximately 400 g, typical of a single-serve soup portion in a Singaporean food-court or casual dining setting.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Tomato soup, canned, condensed (SR Legacy ID 06159) and Seaweed, wakame, raw (SR Legacy ID 11667); values blended and adapted for slow-brewed tomato-seaweed broth
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for brewed tomato seaweed soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy tomato soup (diluted, ~20 kcal/100 g range) and wakame seaweed (raw) proportional blend, consistent with the input record values (energy 20 kcal, protein 0.75 g, fat 0.5 g, carbohydrate 3.25 g per 100 g). Input values retained as they are internally consistent with a dilute vegetable broth. Sodium 200 mg/100 g is plausible for a lightly seasoned broth. All values cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo vegetable soup category for regional plausibility.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Tomato soup (canned, diluted) GI ~38 used as analogous estimate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brewed tomato seaweed soup. GI of 38 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tomato soup (canned, diluted with water), which is the closest approved analogous food. Seaweed addition is expected to lower GI further due to soluble fibre (fucoidan, alginate); GI 38 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (38) × carbohydrate per serving (3.25 g/100 g × 400 g = 13 g) / 100 = 4.94, rounded to 4.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 200 mg per 100 g (800 mg per standard 400 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g serving contributes approximately 800 mg sodium — about 35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association — so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, avoid adding soy sauce, and count this soup toward their daily sodium budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (3.25 g/100 g), low GI (~38), and soluble fibre from seaweed moderating postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic load of approximately 4.9 per 400 g serving and soluble fibre (fucoidan, alginate) from seaweed slowing glucose absorption, this soup is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, any added sugar or starchy thickeners during preparation should be minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.08 g/100 g), zero trans fat, and antioxidant-rich tomato and seaweed ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The negligible saturated fat (0.08 g/100 g), absence of trans fat, and presence of lycopene from tomatoes and omega-3 precursors from seaweed make this soup cardioprotective in a balanced diet; the main cardiovascular caution is the sodium load of 800 mg per bowl, which should be managed in patients with heart failure or salt-sensitive hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols and fucoidan from seaweed; negligible saturated fat; tomato is a nightshade
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Seaweed provides anti-inflammatory fucoidan and polyphenols beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis; however, tomato is a nightshade vegetable and some RA patients report individual sensitivity to solanine — monitor personal response and use low-sodium preparation to avoid sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brewed Tomato Seaweed Soup is a light, savoury vegetable broth slow-brewed from fresh or canned tomatoes and dried or fresh seaweed (typically wakame or kombu). The slow-brewing process extracts natural umami compounds from both the tomato (glutamic acid, lycopene) and the seaweed (glutamates, iodine, fucoidan), producing a deeply flavoured yet low-calorie broth at approximately 20 kcal per 100 g. The texture is thin and clear to lightly cloudy, with a mildly tangy tomato base balanced by the oceanic, mineral notes of seaweed. It is served as a soup course or standalone light meal, commonly in Singaporean food courts, health-focused cafes, and Asian fusion restaurants. Nutritionally, it is very low in fat (0.5 g/100 g), low in carbohydrates (3.25 g/100 g), and provides modest dietary fibre (0.75 g/100 g) from seaweed polysaccharides. Sodium is 200 mg/100 g, yielding approximately 800 mg per standard 400 g bowl — a meaningful contribution to daily sodium intake that warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Clinically, it is well-suited for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and soluble fibre content, and for cardiovascular health due to negligible saturated fat and the presence of lycopene and seaweed antioxidants. The primary clinical caution is sodium management for hypertension and heart failure patients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for brewed tomato seaweed soup exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy tomato soup and wakame seaweed entries, cross-checked against the input record which is internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database tomato soup entry. Confidence is medium rather than low because the input values are plausible and consistent with the adapted source blend.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this slow-brewed tomato-seaweed broth is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or HPB Singapore Foodo as of 2026-04-16; the fat content is very low (0.5 g/100 g) so the absence of fatty acid breakdown does not materially affect clinical utility. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific broth preparation is not reported in the adapted USDA source entries used; estimated ~180 mg/100 g from tomato and seaweed components but not confirmed by an approved source, so -1 is used per numeric hygiene rules. 3. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1 through vitamin_b12, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium) set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific brewed preparation are not available in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, HPB Singapore Foodo) as of 2026-04-16. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — plant-based broth with no animal fats or partially hydrogenated oils; consistent with USDA SR Legacy tomato soup and wakame entries. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — entirely plant-based preparation with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. Ethnic tags 'Ramen', 'Pizza', 'Pasta' from the input record were removed as they are not applicable to this dish. 7. GI of 38 is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may be lower due to seaweed soluble fibre (fucoidan, alginate) content.
